title: Docker Backend: Add conservative deployment-level image retention
description:
## Problem

The canonical service intentionally retains images across jobs and workflows, but the repository has no deployment-level image-maintenance mechanism for bounded disk pressure.

`DockerWorkloadLauncher` and `DockerContainerJanitor` clean containers and do not delete images, which is correct for job cleanup. However, there is no separate image inventory, last-used observation, high/low-watermark policy, operator pin/protected-image set, active-container protection, minimum retention age, dry-run report, or safe eviction loop. On the shared system Docker daemon, an indiscriminate prune would risk deleting operator and MoonMind service images that the container-job service does not own.

Without a conservative maintenance path, the required cross-workflow cache can grow without operator visibility or a governed response to disk pressure.

## Desired behavior

Add a separate deployment-level image-maintenance operation that observes the selected system Docker daemon and only considers images positively attributable to MoonMind container-job use. It remains disabled or dry-run by conservative default, activates eviction only under configured high-water pressure, stops at a low-water target, protects active/recent/pinned/service/unowned images, and emits an operator-reviewable plan/result.

This operation is independent of job/session/workflow cleanup. It never runs `docker system prune -a` or treats an old workflow as authority to delete an image.

## Current evidence

- `moonmind/workloads/docker_launcher.py` removes owned containers but contains no image inventory/retention implementation.
- `DockerContainerJanitor` is label/TTL based for containers/helpers only; it has no disk-watermark or image semantics.
- `docker-compose.yaml` uses one shared system Docker daemon/proxy for MoonMind and potentially other operator workloads.
- #3256 is required to record resolved digest, cache hit, backend scope, and last-used observations for job images.
- #3263 explicitly prohibits image deletion from job cleanup/reconciliation.
- No open or recently closed issue found for conservative deployment-level image maintenance.

## Scope

- Add deployment settings for image maintenance, including:
  - enabled/dry-run flags;
  - polling/schedule interval;
  - disk high-water and low-water thresholds;
  - minimum image age/retention duration;
  - maximum deletions/bytes/time per pass;
  - protected image references/digests and operator pins;
  - optional requirement that an image have a MoonMind last-used record before it is eligible.
- Build a read-only inventory that correlates:
  - backend ref;
  - Docker image ID/repo digests/tags/size/created time;
  - MoonMind last-used observations from #3256;
  - active/stopped container references;
  - protected/pinned images;
  - MoonMind service/runtime images;
  - whether ownership/use is known or ambiguous.
- Compute a deterministic eviction plan only when the high-water threshold is exceeded. Prefer least-recently-used eligible images while enforcing minimum age and protection rules until the low-water target is reached or no safe candidate remains.
- Refuse to delete:
  - images used by active containers;
  - images referenced by MoonMind service containers or currently starting jobs;
  - operator-pinned/protected digests;
  - images younger than minimum retention;
  - unowned/ambiguous images on the system daemon by default;
  - images whose backend scope does not match the selected adapter.
- Execute deletions through a narrowly scoped trusted maintenance Activity/service, re-inspecting immediately before removal to detect races.
- Emit dry-run and execution reports with pressure, candidates, protected/skipped reasons, freed/estimated bytes, failures, and remaining pressure.
- Expose readiness/metrics/operator diagnostics for disk pressure and inability to find safe candidates without turning maintenance into a public agent tool.
- Handle tag/digest aliasing conservatively so removing one candidate does not unexpectedly remove an image still protected by another reference.
- Keep the maintenance schedule separate from per-job workflow cleanup and managed-runtime artifact/workspace retention.

## Out of scope

- `docker system prune -a`, global image prune, or deleting every dangling/unused image.
- Job/session/workflow completion deleting an image.
- Deleting images with ambiguous ownership by default.
- A registry cache/mirror, moving images between daemons, or pre-warming a daemon pool.
- Managing Docker build cache, volumes, arbitrary host files, or non-MoonMind operator resources unless separately designed.
- A dedicated MoonMind daemon or disk-management service.
- Letting agents trigger or configure image eviction.

## Security and authority boundaries

- Deployment operators configure maintenance thresholds and pins; agents/callers cannot alter them.
- The API/job service records non-sensitive image-use observations but does not remove images in request handlers.
- A trusted backend maintenance worker receives Docker authority and must re-check active use/protection immediately before deletion.
- Temporal or the existing durable scheduler owns recurring execution/retry/audit where used.
- Job cleanup has no image-delete authority.
- Agents must not receive Docker endpoint authority, an image-delete tool, operator image inventory beyond authorized diagnostics, or the ability to evict another workload's cache.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Image maintenance is a separate deployment operation from job cleanup and is dry-run/disabled by conservative default.
- [ ] The system observes disk pressure and acts only after the configured high-water threshold is exceeded.
- [ ] Eviction stops at the low-water target, configured pass limit, or absence of safe candidates.
- [ ] Candidate ordering uses backend-scoped last-used observations and minimum retention age.
- [ ] Active-container images, currently starting job images, MoonMind service images, operator pins/protected digests, young images, and ambiguous/unowned images are protected.
- [ ] The service re-inspects candidates immediately before deletion and skips races safely.
- [ ] Tag/digest aliases are handled without deleting a protected image accidentally.
- [ ] Dry-run and execution reports explain every candidate, skip, deletion, failure, and remaining pressure.
- [ ] Job success/failure/cancellation/cleanup never invokes this maintenance path or deletes images.
- [ ] No global prune command is used.
- [ ] Operator visibility reports pressure even when no image can be safely removed.
- [ ] Changing the configured backend isolates inventory/last-used state by backend reference.

## Verification

- Unit tests for watermark validation, candidate eligibility/order, last-used fallback, minimum age, pins, service/active protection, aliasing, pass limits, and dry-run output.
- Docker integration tests with active, stopped, pinned, young, old, tagged, digest-only, shared, MoonMind-used, and unrelated images on the same daemon.
- Race test where a candidate becomes active between planning and deletion; assert it is skipped.
- High/low-water simulation proving maintenance stops at the correct boundary and reports when safe candidates are insufficient.
- Regression test proving normal job cleanup issues no image-delete/prune calls.
- Operator diagnostics/metrics tests for pressure, candidates, freed bytes, skips, failures, and disabled/dry-run modes.
- Scheduler/Activity idempotency tests if recurring execution uses Temporal or the existing startup reconciler.
- Safety test scanning implementation commands/APIs for forbidden global prune behavior.

## Dependencies

- Parent tracker: #3251.
- Prerequisites: #3256 for backend-scoped digest/last-used observations and #3263 for the explicit separation from job cleanup.
- Uses #3254 for trusted image inspect/remove operations and the existing deployment scheduling/worker infrastructure.
- Does not block basic container-job execution, but blocks declaring the shared-cache service operationally complete for long-lived deployments and the disk-pressure validation in the tracker.

## Documentation authority

- `docs/ManagedAgents/DockerBackendService.md` §2 decision 7, §11.5 (deployment-scoped lifetime), §13 (separate maintenance, watermarks, active protection, minimum age, pins, conservative system-daemon behavior), §14 operator observations, §19 rules 13–14, and §§21–22.
- `docs/MoonMindArchitecture.md` sections defining deployment/operator control and trusted worker authority.
- `docs/Temporal/ManagedAndExternalAgentExecutionModel.md` sections governing durable scheduled operations and side-effect ownership.
